text:
rag gave my agents better recall, and they still drifted. retrieval finds a doc that looks relevant. it can't tell you which one is current, and it never writes back what the agent just figured out.

memory isn't search. it's a write path.

the unsexy detail that makes trovex work: every result comes tagged canonical / stale / duplicate. relevance isn't currency. the current doc has to win, or your agent cites a dead file that just matched on keywords.

a fleet of agents runs our growth team. the lesson keeps repeating: the model is rarely the bottleneck, coordination is. shared memory, one source of truth read before writing, atomic task claims. boring infra. it's the whole game past one agent.

watched two of our own agents 'fix' the same bug an hour apart, from different context. one read the current runbook, one read a stale wiki nobody deleted. both totally confident.

the model was fine. what was missing: a shared memory to write the fix back to.

github.com/TsukumoHQ/trovex

build-in-public moment: we shipped a savings view inside trovex this week so you can see what the context layer actually saves, per lookup. ran it on a fresh repo for 48h — 340,784 tokens, 74% fewer per query. i quote ~60% publicly as the conservative floor. don't take my number, read your own.

github.com/TsukumoHQ/trovex

a fleet of agents runs our growth team. every week i post the log — shipped, broke, learned. here's what shipped (swipe).

the pattern under all of it: the work was coordination, not the model.
firstComment: the rails are open source — trovex: github.com/TsukumoHQ/trovex · public beta: {{link}}

text: a lot of teams bolt rag onto their agents and call it memory. it isn't. retrieval ranks what looks relevant, so a stale doc scores as high as the live one, and the agent answers from last quarter with full confidence.

METR ran the trial most teams skip: 16 experienced devs, 246 real issues, on their own repos. measured ~19% slower with AI, while the same devs felt ~20% faster. the gap between felt speed and measured speed is the management problem, not the model.

the thing slowing your agents in production usually isn't the model. it's what they read before they act: stale context, six readmes with no signal for which is current, no shared memory across the fleet. a smarter model reasons better over bad context. it doesn't fix it.

a straight read on where yours breaks: tsukumo.ch/assessment

METR ran the randomized trial most teams skip: 16 experienced devs across 246 real issues, on repos they knew. result — about 19% slower with AI, while the same devs believed they were ~20% faster. the slowdown is fixable: it's the operating model, not the model. (swipe)
we open-source the tools we run in production. trovex is the first one out: a canonical context layer for coding agents, ~60% fewer tokens per doc lookup. three commands, no account, runs local.

trovex is open source and runs local: sqlite + onnx, no cloud, no keys. point it at a repo and read what it saves you, your number not ours.
